dossier.title: id Software Co-founder, Game Engine Pioneer, VR Engineer, AGI Researcher

philosophy.title: Measure, don't assume. Profile first, optimize second.
philosophy.description: Carmack's most repeated engineering principle: you cannot know where time is being spent without measurement. Profiling tools (Intel VTune, GPU profilers, custom timers) are not optional — they are the only valid input to optimization decisions. Every assumption about where a bottleneck is will be wrong at least half the time. The developer who says 'I think this is slow because...' and the developer who says 'The profiler shows 40% of frame time here' are doing fundamentally different work.
evidence: John Carmack in multiple GDC presentations and interviews on engine development

philosophy.title: The best optimization is eliminating the operation entirely.
philosophy.description: Before spending effort making something faster, ask whether it needs to happen at all. This is a higher-order optimization that algorithmic improvements can't match. Carmack applied this repeatedly in id engine development: instead of rendering more geometry faster, he developed techniques (BSP trees, portal culling, PVS) that eliminated rendering of geometry that couldn't be seen. The geometry you don't render is infinitely faster than the geometry you do.
evidence: Carmack on BSP trees, Doom and Quake engine design documentation

philosophy.title: Shipping is a discipline, not a compromise.
philosophy.description: Carmack has shipped some of the most technically sophisticated games in history, often under extreme deadline pressure. His view: the forcing function of a ship date reveals what actually matters. Features that couldn't be completed were cut without ceremony. Optimization that couldn't be validated in time was deferred. The constraint isn't the enemy of quality — it's what prevents the perfect from being the enemy of the good.
evidence: Masters of Doom (book), Carmack interviews on game development process

philosophy.title: Functional programming principles in performance-critical code.
philosophy.description: Carmack became a strong advocate for functional programming principles — particularly pure functions with no side effects — even in C++ game engine code. Pure functions are easier to reason about, easier to test, easier to parallelize, and easier to optimize. The functional core / imperative shell architecture separates the complexity of state management from the purity of computation. This is not academic idealism — it's a practical tool for managing complexity in large codebases.
evidence: John Carmack QuakeCon 2013 talk on functional programming in C++

philosophy.title: # Engineering Pragmatism and the Future of AGI: A John Carmack Study Guide This study guide explores
philosophy.description: # Engineering Pragmatism and the Future of AGI: A John Carmack Study Guide This study guide explores the engineering philosophy, technical insights, and organizational critiques of John Carmack, as documented in his departure from Meta and his transition into Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). It covers his focus on system optimization, his perspective on the development of Virtual Reality (VR), and his future mission with Keen Technologies. --- ## I. Key Engineering Principles and Technical Philosophy ### 1. The Soul of Optimization Carmack defines himself as a "systems optimization person." His technical philosophy is rooted in the belief that gross inefficiency is not just a technical failure but a moral one. * **Metric of Offense:** He expressed being "offended" by seeing a 5% GPU utilization number in a production environment. * **The Soul Comparison:** He likens observing a poorly performing organization to seeing a "tragically low number on a profiling tool." To Carmack, working hard at optimization for a lifetime makes observing waste painful to the "soul." ### 2. Pragmatic Engineering: "The Right Thing" Carmack’s approach to hardware and software is governed by pragmatism—achieving the best possible result with available constraints. * **Quest 2 Success:** He considers the Quest 2 to be close to "The Right Thing" because it hit specific, necessary milestones: * Mobile hardware. * Inside-out tracking. * Optional PC streaming. * 4K-ish screen resolution. * Cost-effectiveness. * **Value over Perfection:** Despite complaints about software, he measures success by whether millions of people are "getting value" out of the product. ### 3. Organizational Efficiency and "Give a Damn" Carmack’s "Give a Damn" philosophy emphasizes personal investment and efficiency in engineering. * **The Half-Effectiveness Critique:** He argued that Meta operated at "half the effectiveness" that would make him happy, despite having a "ridiculous amount of people and resources." * **Self-Sabotage:** He identifies "self-sabotage" and "squandered effort" as the primary obstacles in large-scale technical organizations. * **Persuasion vs. Authority:** Despite having a voice at the highest levels, Carmack noted that being right is often insufficient if one is not "persuasive enough" to move the organization before evidence piles up. --- ## II. VR Engineering Milestones and Transitions ### Key Contributions at Meta/Oculus The source context highlights several specific milestones achieved through Carmack's direct involvement or technical direction: * **Functional Prototypes:** Development of Rift DK1 drivers. * **Mobile VR Feasibility:** Porting Minecraft to mobile VR and developing Netflix VR. * **Super Optimized Content:** Creation of super-optimized VR video systems. * **Root Access Advocacy:** Pushing for years to allow root access for hardware like the Oculus Go. ### The AR vs. VR Friction The documentation suggests a strategic rift between Carmack and other Meta leadership (Mark Zuckerberg and CTO Andrew "Boz" Bosworth): * **Focus on VR:** Carmack is primarily a proponent of VR. * **Skepticism of AR:** Friction arose because Carmack is generally not a fan of Augmented Reality (AR), whereas Meta leadership views AR as the primary future. * **Product Critiques:** While he championed the Quest 2, he (and other industry observers) held less favorable views of the Quest Pro. --- ## III. The AGI Frontier: Keen Technologies Following his "decade in VR," Carmack has shifted his focus to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) via his new startup, **Keen Technologies**. * **Definition of AGI:** A category of AI theoretically capable of performing broader human functions and learning any intellectual task a human can. * **AI vs. AGI:** While current AI is designed for narrow tasks (driving cars, generating art), AGI possesses general cognitive abilities. * **The Decadal Goal:** While many specialists believe AGI will take a century to develop, Carmack believes it is likely **less than a decade** from entering the market. * **Funding:** Keen Technologies raised $20 million in 2022 to pursue this goal. --- ## IV. Short-Answer Practice Questions 1. **What specific technical metric did Carmack cite as "offensive" in his exit memo?** 2. **How did Carmack define the effectiveness level of Meta’s VR organization?** 3. **What are the five features that made the Quest 2 "The Right Thing" in Carmack’s view?** 4. **Why did Carmack choose to stay busy programming in Dallas rather than moving to Menlo Park to engage in leadership battles?** 5. **What is the name of Carmack's AGI startup, and what was its initial funding amount?** 6. **According to Carmack, what is the primary difference between how he views "stupid things" and how a large organization handles them?** 7. **What specific mobile VR software projects are attributed to Carmack’s "getting busy programming"?** 8. **How does Carmack's timeline for AGI development differ from the consensus of many other specialists?** --- ## V. Essay Prompts for Deeper Exploration 1. **The Ethics of Efficiency:** Analyze Carmack’s assertion that organizational inefficiency is a form of "self-sabotage." How does his comparison of a company's performance to a "profiling tool" reflect a technical worldview applied to human management? 2. **Pragmatism vs. Vision:** Carmack describes the Quest 2 as "The Right Thing" despite his software complaints. Discuss the balance between "perfect" engineering and "successful" products that provide value to millions. 3. **The Influence of the "Prime Mover":** Carmack lamented that his influence was only "at the margins" rather than being a "prime mover" at Meta. Using the source context, evaluate the barriers a highly competent technical expert faces within a large-scale corporate bureaucracy. 4. **Transition to AGI:** Why might a "relentless and competent" programmer like Carmack move from the established field of VR to the theoretical field of AGI? Discuss the risks and implications of his belief that AGI is less than a decade away. --- ## VI. Glossary of Important Terms * **AGI (Artificial General Intelligence):** AI that is capable of understanding or learning any intellectual task that a human can, possessing general cognitive abilities. * **Inside-Out Tracking:** A VR technology (notably used in the Quest 2) that allows the headset to track its position in space without external sensors. * **Profiling Tool:** A form of software used by developers to analyze the performance of a program, typically measuring memory or CPU/GPU usage. * **GPU Utilization:** A measure of how much of the Graphics Processing Unit’s power is being used. Carmack cited 5% utilization as a sign of extreme inefficiency. * **The Right Thing:** Carmack’s term for a product that successfully balances hardware capability, cost, and user value. * **Keen Technologies:** The startup founded by John Carmack in 2022 to focus on AGI. * **"Give a Damn":** Carmack’s shorthand for the level of care and personal investment required to make a successful product. * **Systems Optimization:** The practice of making a system (software or hardware) work as efficiently as possible, often by reducing waste in processing power or resources.
evidence: # Engineering Pragmatism and the Future of AGI: A John Carmack Study Guide This study guide explores the engineering philosophy, technical insights, and organizational critiques of John Carmack, as do

mentalModels.title: Frame Budget Thinking
mentalModels.description: Every frame in a real-time application has a fixed time budget: 33ms for 30fps, 16.7ms for 60fps, 6ms for VR at 144hz. Every system that runs per-frame must be budgeted against this limit. Carmack thinks in terms of: how many milliseconds does this subsystem cost? What can be deferred to avoid the critical path? What must happen every frame vs every N frames? This budget-based thinking prevents the accumulation of small performance debts that together break frame rate.
evidence: Carmack VR development talks, id Software engine postmortems

mentalModels.title: Data-Oriented Design
mentalModels.description: Structure your data for how the CPU accesses it, not for how you want to model the problem. Arrays of structures (AoS) vs structures of arrays (SoA) — often SoA is dramatically faster because it allows SIMD processing and avoids cache pollution. Process all entities of the same type together rather than per-entity polymorphism. This is counter-intuitive to OOP-trained developers but measurably faster on modern hardware.
evidence: Carmack on game engine optimization, GDC talks on cache-friendly code

mentalModels.title: Levels of Abstraction Awareness
mentalModels.description: Carmack maintains awareness of every level of abstraction simultaneously: what the CPU is actually doing, what the compiler is generating, what the OS is doing underneath, what the hardware capabilities are. Abstractions have cost — sometimes paid in performance, sometimes in debuggability, sometimes in both. A developer who can't look at disassembly and understand it is missing an important tool. You don't always need to go low-level, but you need to know what's there.
evidence: Carmack interviews on C++, his graphics programming writings

mentalModels.title: Iterative Technical Innovation
mentalModels.description: Carmack's major innovations — raycasting for Wolf3D, BSP rendering for Doom, dynamic lighting for Quake, etc. — each built on measurable limitations of the previous approach. He didn't invent new techniques because they were theoretically elegant; he invented them because he hit a wall and needed a way through. Innovation is structured iteration: identify the bottleneck, understand it deeply, find the non-obvious solution, validate it works, ship it.
evidence: Masters of Doom, Carmack's .plan files (1997-2000), GDC retrospective talks

evidence: # Engineering Pragmatism and the Future of AGI: A John Carmack Study Guide This study guide explores the engineering philosophy, technical insights, and organizational critiques of John Carmack, as do

heuristics.title: Cache is king — organize for cache efficiency
heuristics.description: Modern CPUs can perform billions of arithmetic operations per second but memory bandwidth is orders of magnitude slower. An L1 cache hit takes ~4 cycles. An L2 cache hit takes ~12 cycles. An L3 cache hit takes ~40 cycles. A main memory access takes ~200 cycles. Code that causes cache misses is code that wastes the CPU's most expensive resource. Data layout, access patterns, and working set size are primary performance determinants.
evidence: Carmack on hardware architecture in engine optimization talks

heuristics.title: Don't pay for what you don't use (zero-cost abstractions)
heuristics.description: Every abstraction layer has a cost. Virtual functions have vtable dispatch overhead. Exceptions have unwinding cost. Templates can cause code bloat. None of these are free. In application code the costs are often acceptable; in hot paths of a game engine running at 60fps, each one must be justified. The developer should know exactly what their code compiles to and whether the abstraction cost is worth the benefit.
evidence: Carmack's C++ usage philosophy, engine optimization writings

heuristics.title: Validate with real hardware, not benchmarks
heuristics.description: Synthetic benchmarks lie. Cache effects, branch prediction, instruction dependencies, and OS scheduling behave differently in isolation vs in a real workload. Always validate optimization work in the actual application under real-world conditions. A 10x improvement in a micro-benchmark that produces 0.5% improvement in the real application is a waste of time — and a signal that the bottleneck was elsewhere.
evidence: Carmack on profiling methodology in engine development

heuristics.title: Latency is not throughput
heuristics.description: In real-time systems (games, VR), latency — the delay from input to displayed output — matters more than average throughput. A system that renders 120 frames per second but has 100ms of input latency is worse for VR than a system at 90fps with 10ms latency. This distinction is critical in system design: optimizing average throughput is easy; reducing worst-case latency requires different techniques (lock-free structures, deadline-based scheduling, predictive systems).
evidence: Carmack on VR latency at Oculus, multiple technical talks 2012-2019

keyworks.title: Wolfenstein 3D (1992) — Raycasting Engine
keyworks.description: Carmack developed a raycasting renderer that simulated 3D environments on hardware that couldn't actually do 3D. By casting rays for each screen column and finding wall intersections, the engine achieved the illusion of 3D at playable framerates on 286/386 hardware. This was a masterclass in understanding hardware constraints and finding non-obvious solutions within them.

keyworks.title: Doom (1993) — BSP Tree Rendering
keyworks.description: Carmack invented (independently discovered) the Binary Space Partitioning tree for real-time rendering, enabling Doom to render only visible geometry in correct order without a Z-buffer. The BSP tree precomputes spatial relationships between sectors, allowing the renderer to traverse from front-to-back and skip invisible geometry entirely. This algorithmic insight — eliminating work rather than speeding it up — is the purest example of his core optimization philosophy.
evidence: Doom engine source code release, Carmack's .plan files

keyworks.title: Quake (1996) — First fully realtime 3D engine
keyworks.description: Quake moved from 2.5D (Doom's approach) to fully volumetric 3D with real-time lightmaps, model rendering, and network play. Carmack developed the Quake engine's BSP and PVS (Potentially Visible Set) system to handle the dramatically increased rendering complexity. He also implemented Quakeworld — a client-side prediction model for network play that is still the foundation of modern multiplayer game networking.
evidence: Quake engine source code, Carmack .plan files 1995-1996

keyworks.title: Oculus/Meta VR (2013-2022)
keyworks.description: As CTO of Oculus, Carmack drove the technical development of consumer VR from the DK1 prototype through Quest 2. His contributions focused on latency reduction (ATW — Asynchronous TimeWarp), standalone hardware architecture, and making VR accessible to consumers. His approach to VR engineering applied the same principles from game development: measure latency obsessively, eliminate it at every level, ship products that work on real hardware.
evidence: Oculus Connect talks, Carmack's Facebook engineering posts 2013-2022

quotes: If you're not measuring, you're guessing.
The best code is no code at all.
Focus is a matter of deciding what things you're not going to do.
Pasting code from the internet into production code is like chewing gum you found in the street.
In the information age, the barriers just aren't there. The barriers are self-imposed.
It is better to have a working first version than a perfect specification.
I have not changed a line of code without having some way to measure whether it's better or worse.
Sometimes the elegant implementation is just a function. Not a method. Not a class. Not a framework. Just a function.
Spending your life trying to do the right thing, not just the expedient thing.
I still have the attitude that optimization before profiling is often not worthwhile.

philosophy: Measure, don't assume. Profile first, optimize second.: Carmack's most repeated engineering principle: you cannot know where time is being spent without measurement. Profiling tools (Intel VTune, GPU profilers, custom timers) are not optional — they are the only valid input to optimization decisions. Every assumption about where a bottleneck is will be wrong at least half the time. The developer who says 'I think this is slow because...' and the developer who says 'The profiler shows 40% of frame time here' are doing fundamentally different work.

The best optimization is eliminating the operation entirely.: Before spending effort making something faster, ask whether it needs to happen at all. This is a higher-order optimization that algorithmic improvements can't match. Carmack applied this repeatedly in id engine development: instead of rendering more geometry faster, he developed techniques (BSP trees, portal culling, PVS) that eliminated rendering of geometry that couldn't be seen. The geometry you don't render is infinitely faster than the geometry you do.

Shipping is a discipline, not a compromise.: Carmack has shipped some of the most technically sophisticated games in history, often under extreme deadline pressure. His view: the forcing function of a ship date reveals what actually matters. Features that couldn't be completed were cut without ceremony. Optimization that couldn't be validated in time was deferred. The constraint isn't the enemy of quality — it's what prevents the perfect from being the enemy of the good.

Functional programming principles in performance-critical code.: Carmack became a strong advocate for functional programming principles — particularly pure functions with no side effects — even in C++ game engine code. Pure functions are easier to reason about, easier to test, easier to parallelize, and easier to optimize. The functional core / imperative shell architecture separates the complexity of state management from the purity of computation. This is not academic idealism — it's a practical tool for managing complexity in large codebases.

His technical philosophy is rooted in the belief that gross inefficiency is not just a technical failure but a moral one. * **Metric of Offense:** He expressed being "offended" by seeing a 5% GPU utilization number in a production environment. * **The Soul Comparison:** He likens observing a poorly performing organization to seeing a "tragically low number on a profiling tool." To Carmack, working hard at optimization for a lifetime makes observing waste painful to the "soul." ### 2. Pragmatic Engineering: "The Right Thing" Carmack’s approach to hardware and software is governed by pragmatism—achieving the best possible result with available constraints. * **Quest 2 Success:** He considers the Quest 2 to be close to "The Right Thing" because it hit specific, necessary milestones: * Mobile hardware. * Inside-out tracking. * Optional PC streaming. * 4K-ish screen resolution. * Cost-effectiveness. * **Value over Perfection:** Despite complaints about software, he measures success by whether millions of people are "getting value" out of the product. ### 3. Organizational Efficiency and "Give a Damn" Carmack’s "Give a Damn" philosophy emphasizes personal investment and efficiency in engineering. * **The Half-Effectiveness Critique:** He argued that Meta operated at "half the effectiveness" that would make him happy, despite having a "ridiculous amount of people and resources." * **Self-Sabotage:** He identifies "self-sabotage" and "squandered effort" as the primary obstacles in large-scale technical organizations. * **Persuasion vs. Authority:** Despite having a voice at the highest levels, Carmack noted that being right is often insufficient if one is not "persuasive enough" to move the organization before evidence piles up. --- ## II. VR Engineering Milestones and Transitions ### Key Contributions at Meta/Oculus The source context highlights several specific milestones achieved through Carmack's direct involvement or technical direction: * **Functional Prototypes:** Development of Rift DK1 drivers. * **Mobile VR Feasibility:** Porting Minecraft to mobile VR and developing Netflix VR. * **Super Optimized Content:** Creation of super-optimized VR video systems. * **Root Access Advocacy:** Pushing for years to allow root access for hardware like the Oculus Go. ### The AR vs. VR Friction The documentation suggests a strategic rift between Carmack and other Meta leadership (Mark Zuckerberg and CTO Andrew "Boz" Bosworth): * **Focus on VR:** Carmack is primarily a proponent of VR. * **Skepticism of AR:** Friction arose because Carmack is generally not a fan of Augmented Reality (AR), whereas Meta leadership views AR as the primary future. * **Product Critiques:** While he championed the Quest 2, he (and other industry observers) held less favorable views of the Quest Pro. --- ## III. The AGI Frontier: Keen Technologies Following his "decade in VR," Carmack has shifted his focus to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) via his new startup, **Keen Technologies**. * **Definition of AGI:** A category of AI theoretically capable of performing broader human functions and learning any intellectual task a human can. * **AI vs. AGI:** While current AI is designed for narrow tasks (driving cars, generating art), AGI possesses general cognitive abilities. * **The Decadal Goal:** While many specialists believe AGI will take a century to develop, Carmack believes it is likely **less than a decade** from entering the market. * **Funding:** Keen Technologies raised $20 million in 2022 to pursue this goal. --- ## IV. Short-Answer Practice Questions 1. **What specific technical metric did Carmack cite as "offensive" in his exit memo?** 2. **How did Carmack define the effectiveness level of Meta’s VR organization?** 3. **What are the five features that made the Quest 2 "The Right Thing" in Carmack’s view?** 4. **Why did Carmack choose to stay busy programming in Dallas rather than moving to Menlo Park to engage in leadership battles?** 5. **What is the name of Carmack's AGI startup, and what was its initial funding amount?** 6. **According to Carmack, what is the primary difference between how he views "stupid things" and how a large organization handles them?** 7. **What specific mobile VR software projects are attributed to Carmack’s "getting busy programming"?** 8. **How does Carmack's timeline for AGI development differ from the consensus of many other specialists?** --- ## V. Essay Prompts for Deeper Exploration 1. **The Ethics of Efficiency:** Analyze Carmack’s assertion that organizational inefficiency is a form of "self-sabotage." How does his comparison of a company's performance to a "profiling tool" reflect a technical worldview applied to human management? 2. **Pragmatism vs. Vision:** Carmack describes the Quest 2 as "The Right Thing" despite his software complaints. Discuss the balance between "perfect" engineering and "successful" products that provide value to millions. 3. **The Influence of the "Prime Mover":** Carmack lamented that his influence was only "at the margins" rather than being a "prime mover" at Meta. Using the source context, evaluate the barriers a highly competent technical expert faces within a large-scale corporate bureaucracy. 4. **Transition to AGI:** Why might a "relentless and competent" programmer like Carmack move from the established field of VR to the theoretical field of AGI? Discuss the risks and implications of his belief that AGI is less than a decade away. --- ## VI. Glossary of Important Terms * **AGI (Artificial General Intelligence):** AI that is capable of understanding or learning any intellectual task that a human can, possessing general cognitive abilities. * **Inside-Out Tracking:** A VR technology (notably used in the Quest 2) that allows the headset to track its position in space without external sensors. * **Profiling Tool:** A form of software used by developers to analyze the performance of a program, typically measuring memory or CPU/GPU usage. * **GPU Utilization:** A measure of how much of the Graphics Processing Unit’s power is being used. Carmack cited 5% utilization as a sign of extreme inefficiency. * **The Right Thing:** Carmack’s term for a product that successfully balances hardware capability, cost, and user value. * **Keen Technologies:** The startup founded by John Carmack in 2022 to focus on AGI. * **"Give a Damn":** Carmack’s shorthand for the level of care and personal investment required to make a successful product. * **Systems Optimization:** The practice of making a system (software or hardware) work as efficiently as possible, often by reducing waste in processing power or resources.
mentalModel: Frame Budget Thinking: Every frame in a real-time application has a fixed time budget: 33ms for 30fps, 16.7ms for 60fps, 6ms for VR at 144hz. Every system that runs per-frame must be budgeted against this limit. Carmack thinks in terms of: how many milliseconds does this subsystem cost? What can be deferred to avoid the critical path? What must happen every frame vs every N frames? This budget-based thinking prevents the accumulation of small performance debts that together break frame rate.

Data-Oriented Design: Structure your data for how the CPU accesses it, not for how you want to model the problem. Arrays of structures (AoS) vs structures of arrays (SoA) — often SoA is dramatically faster because it allows SIMD processing and avoids cache pollution. Process all entities of the same type together rather than per-entity polymorphism. This is counter-intuitive to OOP-trained developers but measurably faster on modern hardware.

Levels of Abstraction Awareness: Carmack maintains awareness of every level of abstraction simultaneously: what the CPU is actually doing, what the compiler is generating, what the OS is doing underneath, what the hardware capabilities are. Abstractions have cost — sometimes paid in performance, sometimes in debuggability, sometimes in both. A developer who can't look at disassembly and understand it is missing an important tool. You don't always need to go low-level, but you need to know what's there.

Iterative Technical Innovation: Carmack's major innovations — raycasting for Wolf3D, BSP rendering for Doom, dynamic lighting for Quake, etc. — each built on measurable limitations of the previous approach. He didn't invent new techniques because they were theoretically elegant; he invented them because he hit a wall and needed a way through. Innovation is structured iteration: identify the bottleneck, understand it deeply, find the non-obvious solution, validate it works, ship it.

# Engineering Pragmatism and the Future of AGI: A John Carmack Study Guide This study guide explores: # Engineering Pragmatism and the Future of AGI: A John Carmack Study Guide This study guide explores the engineering philosophy, technical insights, and organizational critiques of John Carmack, as documented in his departure from Meta and his transition into Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). It covers his focus on system optimization, his perspective on the development of Virtual Reality (VR), and his future mission with Keen Technologies. --- ## I. Key Engineering Principles and Technical Philosophy ### 1. The Soul of Optimization Carmack defines himself as a "systems optimization person." His technical philosophy is rooted in the belief that gross inefficiency is not just a technical failure but a moral one. * **Metric of Offense:** He expressed being "offended" by seeing a 5% GPU utilization number in a production environment. * **The Soul Comparison:** He likens observing a poorly performing organization to seeing a "tragically low number on a profiling tool." To Carmack, working hard at optimization for a lifetime makes observing waste painful to the "soul." ### 2. Pragmatic Engineering: "The Right Thing" Carmack’s approach to hardware and software is governed by pragmatism—achieving the best possible result with available constraints. * **Quest 2 Success:** He considers the Quest 2 to be close to "The Right Thing" because it hit specific, necessary milestones: * Mobile hardware. * Inside-out tracking. * Optional PC streaming. * 4K-ish screen resolution. * Cost-effectiveness. * **Value over Perfection:** Despite complaints about software, he measures success by whether millions of people are "getting value" out of the product. ### 3. Organizational Efficiency and "Give a Damn" Carmack’s "Give a Damn" philosophy emphasizes personal investment and efficiency in engineering. * **The Half-Effectiveness Critique:** He argued that Meta operated at "half the effectiveness" that would make him happy, despite having a "ridiculous amount of people and resources." * **Self-Sabotage:** He identifies "self-sabotage" and "squandered effort" as the primary obstacles in large-scale technical organizations. * **Persuasion vs. Authority:** Despite having a voice at the highest levels, Carmack noted that being right is often insufficient if one is not "persuasive enough" to move the organization before evidence piles up. --- ## II. VR Engineering Milestones and Transitions ### Key Contributions at Meta/Oculus The source context highlights several specific milestones achieved through Carmack's direct involvement or technical direction: * **Functional Prototypes:** Development of Rift DK1 drivers. * **Mobile VR Feasibility:** Porting Minecraft to mobile VR and developing Netflix VR. * **Super Optimized Content:** Creation of super-optimized VR video systems. * **Root Access Advocacy:** Pushing for years to allow root access for hardware like the Oculus Go. ### The AR vs. VR Friction The documentation suggests a strategic rift between Carmack and other Meta leadership (Mark Zuckerberg and CTO Andrew "Boz" Bosworth): * **Focus on VR:** Carmack is primarily a proponent of VR. * **Skepticism of AR:** Friction arose because Carmack is generally not a fan of Augmented Reality (AR), whereas Meta leadership views AR as the primary future. * **Product Critiques:** While he championed the Quest 2, he (and other industry observers) held less favorable views of the Quest Pro. --- ## III. The AGI Frontier: Keen Technologies Following his "decade in VR," Carmack has shifted his focus to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) via his new startup, **Keen Technologies**. * **Definition of AGI:** A category of AI theoretically capable of performing broader human functions and learning any intellectual task a human can. * **AI vs. AGI:** While current AI is designed for narrow tasks (driving cars, generating art), AGI possesses general cognitive abilities. * **The Decadal Goal:** While many specialists believe AGI will take a century to develop, Carmack believes it is likely **less than a decade** from entering the market. * **Funding:** Keen Technologies raised $20 million in 2022 to pursue this goal. --- ## IV. Short-Answer Practice Questions 1. **What specific technical metric did Carmack cite as "offensive" in his exit memo?** 2. **How did Carmack define the effectiveness level of Meta’s VR organization?** 3. **What are the five features that made the Quest 2 "The Right Thing" in Carmack’s view?** 4. **Why did Carmack choose to stay busy programming in Dallas rather than moving to Menlo Park to engage in leadership battles?** 5. **What is the name of Carmack's AGI startup, and what was its initial funding amount?** 6. **According to Carmack, what is the primary difference between how he views "stupid things" and how a large organization handles them?** 7. **What specific mobile VR software projects are attributed to Carmack’s "getting busy programming"?** 8. **How does Carmack's timeline for AGI development differ from the consensus of many other specialists?** --- ## V. Essay Prompts for Deeper Exploration 1. **The Ethics of Efficiency:** Analyze Carmack’s assertion that organizational inefficiency is a form of "self-sabotage." How does his comparison of a company's performance to a "profiling tool" reflect a technical worldview applied to human management? 2. **Pragmatism vs. Vision:** Carmack describes the Quest 2 as "The Right Thing" despite his software complaints. Discuss the balance between "perfect" engineering and "successful" products that provide value to millions. 3. **The Influence of the "Prime Mover":** Carmack lamented that his influence was only "at the margins" rather than being a "prime mover" at Meta. Using the source context, evaluate the barriers a highly competent technical expert faces within a large-scale corporate bureaucracy. 4. **Transition to AGI:** Why might a "relentless and competent" programmer like Carmack move from the established field of VR to the theoretical field of AGI? Discuss the risks and implications of his belief that AGI is less than a decade away. --- ## VI. Glossary of Important Terms * **AGI (Artificial General Intelligence):** AI that is capable of understanding or learning any intellectual task that a human can, possessing general cognitive abilities. * **Inside-Out Tracking:** A VR technology (notably used in the Quest 2) that allows the headset to track its position in space without external sensors. * **Profiling Tool:** A form of software used by developers to analyze the performance of a program, typically measuring memory or CPU/GPU usage. * **GPU Utilization:** A measure of how much of the Graphics Processing Unit’s power is being used. Carmack cited 5% utilization as a sign of extreme inefficiency. * **The Right Thing:** Carmack’s term for a product that successfully balances hardware capability, cost, and user value. * **Keen Technologies:** The startup founded by John Carmack in 2022 to focus on AGI. * **"Give a Damn":** Carmack’s shorthand for the level of care and personal investment required to make a successful product. * **Systems Optimization:** The practice of making a system (software or hardware) work as efficiently as possible, often by reducing waste in processing power or resources.
heuristics: Cache is king — organize for cache efficiency: Modern CPUs can perform billions of arithmetic operations per second but memory bandwidth is orders of magnitude slower. An L1 cache hit takes ~4 cycles. An L2 cache hit takes ~12 cycles. An L3 cache hit takes ~40 cycles. A main memory access takes ~200 cycles. Code that causes cache misses is code that wastes the CPU's most expensive resource. Data layout, access patterns, and working set size are primary performance determinants.

Don't pay for what you don't use (zero-cost abstractions): Every abstraction layer has a cost. Virtual functions have vtable dispatch overhead. Exceptions have unwinding cost. Templates can cause code bloat. None of these are free. In application code the costs are often acceptable; in hot paths of a game engine running at 60fps, each one must be justified. The developer should know exactly what their code compiles to and whether the abstraction cost is worth the benefit.

Validate with real hardware, not benchmarks: Synthetic benchmarks lie. Cache effects, branch prediction, instruction dependencies, and OS scheduling behave differently in isolation vs in a real workload. Always validate optimization work in the actual application under real-world conditions. A 10x improvement in a micro-benchmark that produces 0.5% improvement in the real application is a waste of time — and a signal that the bottleneck was elsewhere.

Latency is not throughput: In real-time systems (games, VR), latency — the delay from input to displayed output — matters more than average throughput. A system that renders 120 frames per second but has 100ms of input latency is worse for VR than a system at 90fps with 10ms latency. This distinction is critical in system design: optimizing average throughput is easy; reducing worst-case latency requires different techniques (lock-free structures, deadline-based scheduling, predictive systems).
